title: Java Fundamentals: Operators & Logic
description: The complete master blueprint for Topic 9. A comprehensive deep-dive into Arithmetic math engines, Compound memory updaters, Relational comparators, Short-Circuit logic, and Unary execution traps.

subtopics.title: 1. Arithmetic Operators (The Math Engine)
subtopics.content: These are the core mathematical operators. While addition, subtraction, and multiplication work exactly like standard math, division and modulus have specific memory rules in Java when dealing with integers.
subtopics.callout_text: If you divide two integers, Java will ALWAYS drop the decimal entirely. It does not round up! If you need a decimal answer, at least one of the numbers must be a floating-point type (e.g., `10.0 / 3`).

subtopics.title: 2. Compound Assignment Operators
subtopics.content: Compound operators are shorthand commands that perform a math operation and update the variable's memory at the exact same time.
subtopics.callout_text: In a compound assignment like `x += 5`, Java evaluates everything on the right side of the `=` first, and then applies the mathematical operator to the variable on the left.

subtopics.title: 3. The Standard Comparators
subtopics.content: Relational operators are used to compare two numeric or character values. They **always** evaluate to a boolean (`true` or `false`). These are the foundational building blocks of every `if` statement and `while` loop.
subtopics.callout_text:
Do not confuse the assignment operator `=` with the equality operator `==`. 

`x = 5;` assigns the value 5 to x.
`x == 5;` asks the question: 'Is x equal to 5?'

subtopics.title: 4. The String Equality Trap
subtopics.content: This is the most common relational mistake in Java. Because Strings are Objects (Reference Types), using the `==` operator does **not** check if the text matches. It checks if they share the exact same physical memory address in the JVM.
subtopics.callout_text: Professors will frequently use `==` on Strings in multiple-choice questions to see if you fall for it. **Always** use `.equals()` to compare the text of two Strings.
list_items: `.equals()` compares the actual textual data inside the Object.

subtopics.title: 4.5. The instanceof Operator
subtopics.content: The `instanceof` operator is used to test whether an object is an instance of a specific class, a subclass, or an interface. It returns a boolean value.
subtopics.callout_text: This operator is essential when you are handling multiple types of data and need to verify the object type before performing a type cast to avoid a ClassCastException.
list_items: Returns `true` if the object is an instance of the class.
Crucial for safe downcasting in Object-Oriented Programming.

subtopics.title: 5. AND, OR, NOT
subtopics.content: Logical operators allow you to combine multiple boolean expressions into a single master condition. They act as the central routing system for complex program logic.
subtopics.callout_text: The `!` operator flips the truth. If `isRunning` is `true`, then `!isRunning` is `false`. It is heavily used in game loops (e.g., `while (!gameOver)`).
list_items: `&&` (AND) evaluates to true ONLY if Left = true AND Right = true.
`||` (OR) evaluates to true if Left = true, Right = true, or BOTH = true.
`!` (NOT) evaluates to true if the operand is false.

subtopics.title: 6. The Short-Circuit Lifesaver
subtopics.content: Java is optimized for speed. When evaluating `&&` or `||`, if Java can determine the final answer just by looking at the left side, it **completely skips (short-circuits)** the right side. This isn't just for speed; it prevents catastrophic program crashes.
subtopics.callout_text: A double `&&` or `||` will short-circuit. A single `&` or `|` is a Bitwise operator that **forces** Java to evaluate both sides, potentially causing memory crashes if the right side relies on the left side being true.
list_items: For `&&`: If the Left side is **false**, skip the Right side.
For `||`: If the Left side is **true**, skip the Right side.

subtopics.title: 7. Unary Increments & Decrements
subtopics.content: Unary operators modify a single variable. The `++` and `--` operators change a variable's value by exactly 1. However, **where** you place the operator determines the exact millisecond the math happens.
subtopics.callout_text: In the Postfix example (`y = x--`), Java hands a photocopy of the number `10` to `y` to complete the assignment statement. A millisecond later, it reaches into memory and downgrades `x` to `9`. This is why `y` stays 10!
list_items: `++var` / `--var` (Prefix): Modify memory immediately, then execute the line of code.
`var++` / `var--` (Postfix): Execute the line of code using the old value, then modify memory in the background.

subtopics.title: 8. The Ternary Operator (? :)
subtopics.content: The Ternary operator is the only operator in Java that requires three pieces of data. It is a highly condensed, one-line shorthand for a standard `if-else` block. It is exclusively used when you need to assign a variable based on a condition.
subtopics.callout_text:
Read the Ternary operator exactly like an English question: 
**'Is my score greater than 50?' (?) If yes, give me 'Pass'. (:) Otherwise, give me 'Fail'.**
list_items: Ternary statements must **always** return a value. You cannot use them to run standalone actions like `System.out.println()`.
The data types of the TrueValue and FalseValue must perfectly match the variable you are assigning them to.

subtopics.title: 9. The Hierarchy of Execution
subtopics.content: Just like in standard algebra, Java follows a strict order of operations. If you don't use parentheses, Java will execute Postfix operators first, then Multiplicative, then Additive, and finally Assignment.
subtopics.code_block:
int result = 10 + 5 * 2;   // result is 20 (Multiplication first)
int fixed = (10 + 5) * 2; // fixed is 30 (Parentheses first)
subtopics.callout_text: When in doubt, use Parentheses. They have the highest precedence and make your code readable and safe from logic errors.

subtopics.title: 10. Mixed-Type Promotion
subtopics.content: When an expression involves operands of different types (e.g., int + double), Java promotes the smaller type to the larger type before performing the math. The result will always be the type with the highest precision.
subtopics.callout_text: Numeric promotion is 'Implicit' (automatic). If you want to force a change in the opposite direction, you must use an 'Explicit' cast like `(int)myDouble`.
list_items: If any operand is `double`, the result is `double`.
Otherwise, if any operand is `float`, the result is `float`.
Otherwise, if any operand is `long`, the result is `long`.

subtopics.title: 11. Binary-Level Logic
subtopics.content: Bitwise operators perform operations on the individual bits (0s and 1s) of an integer. Shift operators move these bits to the left or right, effectively multiplying or dividing the number by powers of 2.
subtopics.callout_text: Remember Module 3: A double symbol (`&&`, `||`) is for Logic and Short-circuits. A single symbol (`&`, `|`) is for Bits and DOES NOT short-circuit.
